Psalm 87 announces something revolutionary: belonging to Zion, the city of God, transcends every earthly identity. The psalmist doesn't celebrate Jerusalem as a political or military power. He celebrates it as the place where the Lord has chosen to dwell, where He has founded His city on the holy mountains. The gate of Zion matters more to God than all the dwellings of Jacob combined—a staggering claim that the place of His presence outweighs the entire nation. This isn't sentiment. This is theology about what truly deserves human allegiance and love. The psalmist is asking: where is your heart oriented? Toward the temporary securities of earthly dwelling, or toward the city where God Himself is present?

Then comes the stunning turnaround. The Lord will claim for Himself people from the nations that stood against Israel and opposed His purposes. Rahab represents Egypt—the ancient oppressor. Babylon would become the future captors. Philistia, Tyre, and Cush were foreign powers, some hostile, some simply outside the covenant community. Yet God says of each one He chooses: "This one was born in Zion." Birth in Zion is not about geography or parentage. It's about spiritual origin and identity. When the Most High establishes someone in His city, their true citizenship is determined. They belong to Zion not because of where they came from, but because of where God has claimed them.

The image of God recording names in a register cuts to the heart of what matters. Human records track wealth, power, and lineage. God's register tracks only one thing: membership in Zion. This is the citizenship that endures. Every other credential becomes secondary when compared to being written in the register of the peoples as one born in Zion. The psalmist sees a future where celebration fills the city—singers and pipers proclaiming that all springs of joy flow from the Lord. This is not mere religious enthusiasm. This is the deep satisfaction that comes from being established by the Most High in the place where He dwells.

For those reading this now, Psalm 87 demands a question: where is your truest home? What citizenship captures your ultimate loyalty? The physical city of Jerusalem has given way to Christ Himself, who embodied and fulfilled all that Zion represented. To be born in Zion now means to be born again in Christ—to be established in Him by the work of the Holy Spirit. That registration in God's record, that being claimed as belonging to His city, is available to you. But it demands that you reorient what you love most. It demands that you recognize all other dwellings, all other securities, as secondary to belonging to Christ. The question remains: will you let yourself be claimed by the God who loves His city more than anything else?
